coredata.dc:title: Coordinated carbon dots-Fe featuring synergistically enhanced peroxidase-like activity at neutral pH for specific and background-free detection of dichlorvos
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yangyu
Xu, Jinju
Zhou, Rongrong
Qin, Ziyi
Liao, Chunhui
Shi, Shuyun
Chen, Yuxia
Guo, Ying
Zhang, Shuihan
coredata.dc:description: Nanozyme-based assays present favorable prospects in sensitive determination of pesticides in food samples yet are constrained with little or even no catalytic activities at neutral pH, lack of selectivity, and interference by sample color. Here, a facile and novel photothermal system for rapid, specific, sensitive, and background-free detection of dichlorvos was developed based on peroxidase-like activity of coordinated carbon dots-Fe (CDs-Fe). CDs-Fe was prepared and fabricated based on the redox and coordination chemistry between CDs and Fe species. Catalytic mechanism investigations illustrate that CDs-Fe with outstanding peroxidase-like activity at neutral pH comes from the synergistic effect of •OH production and electron transfer process. More interestingly, dichlorvos can specifically complex with CDs-Fe through electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interactions, which then cement substrates affinity and improve electron transfer efficiency to enhance the catalytic activity. Oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (oxTMB) presents photothermal effect by absorption of near-infrared (808 nm) laser-driven light, which eliminates the color interference from real samples. Accordingly, a background-free photothermal strategy was established. Furthermore, a portable hydrogel kit was fabricated by embedding CDs-Fe and TMB into agarose hydrogel for on-site rapid detection of dichlorvos. A good relationship was observed in the range of 5.00–650 μg L−1 with a satisfactory detection limit at 4.85 μg L−1, and a good recovery (90.56–107.0 %) with excellent anti-interference capacity was achieved for real sample analysis. The work presents a novel nanozyme enhancement strategy for specific and background-free detecting dichlorvos in food samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozyme-based assays present favorable prospects in sensitive determination of pesticides in food samples yet are constrained with little or even no catalytic activities at neutral pH, lack of selectivity, and interference by sample color. Here, a facile and novel photothermal system for rapid, specific, sensitive, and background-free detection of dichlorvos was developed based on peroxidase-like activity of coordinated carbon dots-Fe (CDs-Fe). CDs-Fe was prepared and fabricated based on the redox and coordination chemistry between CDs and Fe species. Catalytic mechanism investigations illustrate that CDs-Fe with outstanding peroxidase-like activity at neutral pH comes from the synergistic effect of •OH production and electron transfer process. More interestingly, dichlorvos can specifically complex with CDs-Fe through electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interactions, which then cement substrates affinity and improve electron transfer efficiency to enhance the catalytic activity. Oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (oxTMB) presents photothermal effect by absorption of near-infrared (808 nm) laser-driven light, which eliminates the color interference from real samples. Accordingly, a background-free photothermal strategy was established. Furthermore, a portable hydrogel kit was fabricated by embedding CDs-Fe and TMB into agarose hydrogel for on-site rapid detection of dichlorvos. A good relationship was observed in the range of 5.00–650 μg L−1 with a satisfactory detection limit at 4.85 μg L−1, and a good recovery (90.56–107.0 %) with excellent anti-interference capacity was achieved for real sample analysis. The work presents a novel nanozyme enhancement strategy for specific and background-free detecting dichlorvos in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides are indispensable chemical compounds in agricultural production to protect crops against pests and insects efficiently [1–3], among which dichlorvos (2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate) is widely used owing to its low price and high efficiency [4]. However, the excessive or irrational use makes humans being easily exposed to dichlorvos via the ingestion of contaminated water/food as well as the inhalation of contaminated air [5,6], resulting in a serious threat to many organs/systems (e.g., kidney, liver, nervous system, respiratory system, reproductive system) [7,8]. Because of the high toxicity, the World Health Organization has classified dichlorvos in group IIb, and many locations/National Standards have set the maximum residue limits of dichlorvos in water (10 μg L−1) and vegetables/fruits (200 μg kg−1) [9,10]. Therefore, development of simple, rapid, reliable, and sensitive methods for dichlorvos residue monitoring are imperative and urgent.

So far, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing strategies for dichlorvos detection, including liquid/gas chromatography coupling with mass spectrometry [11], fluorescent [12,13], colorimetric [14,15], electrochemistry [16], and surface-enhanced Raman scattering analysis [17]. By contrast, fluorescent and colorimetric biosensing are among the most popular and dominant strategies due to their superior advantages of high sensitivity, rapid analysis, simple operation, on-site detection, and reliable results [18–20]. Currently, most of the developed dichlorvos biosensing strategies are using bioenzymes (e.g., acetylcholinesterase, AChE; butylcholinesterase; esterase) as biorecognition elements [1,21]. Dichlorvos strongly and irreversibly inhibits the activity of AChE and then suppresses the production of thiocholine, which modulates the generation of detectable fluorescent or colorimetric signals in the cascade (bio)chemical reaction [22]. As far as we are aware, bioenzyme-inhibition based biosensors pave the way for a speedy and feasible detection of dichlorvos by bypassing the copious pre-treatments. Unfortunately, bioenzymes are unstable, costly, and the non-specific enzyme inhibition of pesticides are not conducive to the specific detection, consequently limiting their practical applications [15]. Another problem is that the prevalence of matrix interferences in food samples, such as pigments (e.g., chlorophyl, carotene) with colorimetric and fluorescent signals, will affect the accurate and sensitive detection, easily leading to false-positive results [22,23]. Thus, bioenzyme-free, background-free, and specific strategies for dichlorvos detection are the significant challenges remaining in real samples analysis.

Development of robust enzyme mimics is considered as an ideal solution to make up for the drawbacks of bioenzymes [1]. Nanozymes with unique enzymatic functions and nanomaterials properties have opened new prospects because of their significant advantages over bioenzymes, such as easy storage, high stability, facile synthesis, excellent reproducibility, cost effectiveness, and adjustable catalytic activity [24–26]. Peroxidase/oxidase-like nanozymes can catalyze chromogenic substrates (e.g., 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, TMB; o-phenylenediamine) to produce colored and/or fluorescent products under the H2O2/O2 participation, which as a result can offer the catalytic amplification of signals for highly sensitive detection [22,27]. To date, a series of nanozymes have been elaborately designed and widely utilized in pesticides analysis [1]. In fact, how to realize specific pesticides detection is still a vital problem in nanozyme-based detection systems. Usually, nanozymes is combined with other biomimetic recognition elements (e.g., molecular imprinted polymers, antibody, aptamer) to improve specificity [1,28]. However, interactions between nanozymes and biomimetic recognition elements is more complex. An interesting but rarely concerned point is the direct and specific effect on the activity of nanozymes by pesticides, which can provide more accessibility for designing facile, rapid, specific, and sensitive assays [21]. Zhang et al. proposed a colorimetric method for specific detection of thiophanate-methyl based on its strong affinity to copper-doped carbon nanozyme to inhibit its oxidase-like activity at pH 4.0 [29]. Tai et al. constructed a colorimetric assay for glyphosate based on its direct and specific inhibition towards peroxidase-like activity of β-cyclodextrin@DNA-copper nanoclusters enzyme mimics with working pH at 4.0 [30]. In order to decrease false positive results in practice, research on nanozyme-based enhancement by pesticides presents bright prospect [31]. Dimethoate enhances the oxidase-like activity of Ag2O (pH 5.0), and then promotes the catalytic oxidation of TMB for specific colorimetric detection of dimethoate [31]. As was well-known, almost all of the reported nanozymes exhibit little or even no catalytic activities at neutral pH and need to work in acidic conditions (pH 3.0–5.0) [32]. Some pesticides were unstable and can be hydrolyzed in the acidic medium [20,33], which will result in quantitative errors. Moreover, multiple operation for precise pH adjustment can lead to material waste, increased operation time, and secondary pollution [32]. Thus, it is highly desirable to fabricate nanozymes with well activity at neutral pH, and the activity can be directly enhanced by pesticides for specific and accurate detection.

Recently, the photothermal analysis platforms based on temperature signal readouts have been extensively explored because of its simplicity, convenience, and on-site practicality in different sensing field [23,34,35]. Interestingly, besides superior absorbance in the UV–visible spectrum for colorimetric analysis, oxidized TMB (oxTMB) also exhibits absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) spectrum and presents strong NIR laser (808 nm)-triggered photoconversion efficiency for quantitative photothermal detection with high sensitivity [23]. We previously reported that plant pigments (e.g., chlorophyll) often have UV–visible absorption and fluorescent emission, which sometimes severely interfered the detection results [22]. Evidently, background-free detection of pesticides in real samples can be realized using oxTMB as photothermal reagent to translate detection information into heat signal, which can resolve the limitation of conventional colorimetric and fluorescent sensing systems.

Taking the above into account, we fabricated a photothermal sensor system based on synergistically enhanced peroxidase-like activity of coordinated carbon dots-Fe (CDs-Fe) at neutral pH for sensitive, accurate, specific, and background-free detection of dichlorvos (Scheme 1 ). CDs with oxygen and nitrogen-rich functional groups derived from aloe leaves can react with Fe3+ through redox and coordination chemistry to prepare CDs-Fe with Fe3+/Fe2+ species. The obtained CDs-Fe present excellent peroxidase-like activity under neutral pH by synergistical roles of electron transfer process and generation of •OH. Interestingly, CDs-Fe displays a direct and specific response to dichlorvos through electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interactions, and subsequent enhancement of intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, contributing to a promising alternative for simple, rapid, specific, sensitive, and background-free photothermal detection of dichlorvos. Furthermore, we developed a photothermal hydrogel detection platform by loading CDs-Fe and TMB into agarose hydrogel for on-site analysis of real samples.
Details of materials and apparatus were described in Electronic Supplementary Materials.

CDs were firstly synthesized using natural biomass, aloe leaves, as the starting material according to our previous report with minor modifications [20]. Briefly, smashed fresh aloe leaves (40 g), NaOH aqueous solution (1.0 mol L−1, 6 mL), and anhydrous ethanol (94 mL) were mixed in a round-bottom flask (250 mL) and refluxed at 95 °C for 24 h under nitrogen protection. After being cooled down to room temperature, the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane, and re-transferred into the round-bottom flask, which was then allowed to be dropped with FeCl3 aqueous solution (1.0 mol L−1, 150 μL). Subsequently, HCl (1 mol L−1, 6 mL) was introduced to adjusted pH of the solution till the precipitate was dissolved. Then, the mixture was magnetically stirred at 600 rpm at 25 °C for 3 h, dialyzed (500 Da) against deionized water for 3 days, and then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the resulting CDs-Fe was obtained by freeze-drying and stored at 4 °C for further use.
For comparison, CDs, CDs-metal ion, and Fe doped CDs (FeCDs) were prepared, and the detailed procedures were presented in Electronic Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe and CDs-Fe/dichlorvos
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like catalytic activities of CDs-Fe and CDs-Fe/dichlorvos were investigate using TMB as the chromogenic substrate. In brief, CDs-Fe (6 μL, 2 mg mL−1) or CDs-Fe/dichlorvos (6 μL, 2 mg mL−1), TMB (10 μL, 10 mM), and H2O2 (10 μL, 100 mM) were mixed with ultrapure water (174 μL) and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. Then, the UV–vis spectra and absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm were recorded at room temperature.

The Steady-state kinetic study was carried out using the same steps as those mentioned above with fixing concentration of TMB (0.5 mM) or H2O2 (5 mM) and adding various concentrations of H2O2 or TMB, respectively. The absorbance at 652 nm was measured at different time intervals via a time scan mode. The double-reciprocal curves TMB and H2O2 were plotted according to the Michaelis‒Menten equation:
1 v

Where v and v max denote the initial and the maximal reaction velocity, K m represents the Michaelis constant, [S] is the concentration of substrates.

A photothermal hydrogel detection platform was fabricated. Initially, agarose powder (50 mg) was dissolved in H2O (10 mL) and kept stirring in boiling water until being fully dissolved. After that, CDs-Fe (50 μL, 6 mg mL−1) and TMB (50 μL, 100 mM) were added and mixed. Subsequently, 170 μL of the mixed solution were quickly dropped to 96-well microplates in turn and cooling down to room temperature. The prepared hydrogel kit was then stored at 4 °C for further use.

For dichlorvos detection, various amounts of dichlorvos (20.0 μL, 0.05–7.0 mg L−1) and 10 μL of H2O2 (100 mM) were dropped into the hydrogel kit in succession and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. After reaction, the hydrogel kit was exposed to NIR laser light irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2) for 2.5 min. The temperature changes (ΔT) were monitored using the thermal imaging camera. Finally, the relationships between ΔT and dichlorvos concentrations were established for detection.

Food samples (i.e., lettuce leaves, spinach leaves) were bought from local supermarket (Changsha, China) and employed for real sample assays. Fresh sample (100.0 g) was sprayed with different concentrations of dichlorvos, chopped, and mixed with acetone:water (2:1, v/v, 200 mL). Subsequently, samples were extracted by homogenizer (10000 r min−1) for 1 min and centrifuged (8000 r min−1) for 5 min (GB 23200.8–2016). Thereafter, the resulting supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane, concentrated in a vacuum drier, and redissolved with acetone:water solution (2:1, v/v, 1.0 mL). Finally, extract solution (10 μL) was dropped into the hydrogel kit for incubation at 37 °C for 20 min, irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2) for 2.5 min, collection of thermal images, and analysis of spiked amounts by standard curve method. Three parallel experiments were performed for each sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs-Fe nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: CDs play crucial roles for fabricating novel functional nanomaterials [36,37], and it is reported that coupling metal ions or metal nanoparticles with CDs could achieve higher nanozymatic catalytic properties due to the charge transfer at the metal-carbon interface and the facilitation of substrate adsorption [38]. Fe, an essential element for humans with low-cost and nontoxic property, has been selected as an appropriate metal to fabricate a variety of nanozymes (e.g., Fe3O4/Fe2O3 nanozymes and their composites, Fe single-atom nanozymes, ferric salts, ferric heteroatom compounds) with unique structural and physicochemical properties [26]. While for CDs, a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials has become “hotspots” in nanozymes field because of their outstanding physicochemical properties, such as easy and low-cost preparation process, prominent optical characteristics, excellent biocompatibility, and fast electron mobility [39]. To date, studies focused on the fabrication of Fe doping into the carbonized structure (FeCDs) and investigation of their peroxidase-like activity in acidic conditions [26,40]. We previously found that CDs with plenty of surface oxygen and nitrogen functional groups could present reducing ability to metal ions [41] and coordinate with Fe with high affinity [42]. Thus, it is reasonable that interaction between reducing CDs and Fe3+ will fabricate a new and unique CDs-Fe nanozyme with Fe3+/Fe2+ species.

Here, CDs were synthesized using natural biomass, aloe leaves, as the starting material, which were expected to bind with Fe3+/Fe2+ species based on their redox and coordination chemistry to fabricate CDs-Fe nanozyme. To confirm the structure and composition of CDs-Fe, transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were performed. TEM image reveals that spherical-shaped CDs-Fe is well-dispersed with a narrow size distribution (average diameter of 100 random nanoparticles, 5.14 nm) (Fig. 1 A). For CDs, a similar morphology with a smaller particle size can be observed (average diameter of 100 random nanoparticles, 3.52 nm) (Fig. S1A). High-resolution images of CDs-Fe and CDs exhibits the same ordered lattice plane of 0.21 nm (Insets in Fig. 1A and S1A), assigning to the (100) characteristic face of sp2-graphite carbon [20]. Thus, it can be concluded that CDs-Fe has the same crystallographic core with that of CDs. This deduction can also be verified by the broad diffraction peak with a center position at around 23.7° observed in XRD patterns for both CDs and CDs-Fe (Fig. S1B). Afterward, FTIR was applied to uncover the distribution of surface functional groups. As depicted in Fig. 1B, bands at 3233, 2922/2839, 1725, 1578, 1366, and 1076/1034 cm−1 are contributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H/O–H, C–H, CO, CC/CN, C–N, and C–O–C groups. By comparison with CDs, a new peak at 548 cm−1 is observed for CDs-Fe (Fig. 1B), suggesting the existence of Fe–O bond [26]. Simultaneously, relative high intensity of stretching vibration peak for carboxyl group at 1725 cm−1 in CDs-Fe reveals the oxidization of hydroxyl group to carboxyl group by Fe3+ [41]. In addition, by comparison with the FTIR spectra of CDs (Fig. 2 B), red-shifted signals for CDs-Fe provide evidence that effective multiple interactions happen between Fe and surface oxygen and nitrogen-rich functional groups of CDs-Fe. Surface state and composition of CDs-Fe were further analyzed by XPS investigation. The full survey scanned XPS spectrum of CDs-Fe shows the presence of C1s (61.9 %), N1s (1.78 %), O1s (35.3 %), and Fe2p (1.02 %) orbitals, corresponding to peaks at 286.1, 400.1, 533.1, 712.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 1C). The relative higher atomic percentage of O, the occurrence of Fe element, and the increased CO content (27.5 %) in CDs-Fe (Fig. 1C and S1C) clearly suggest the oxidation of CDs to produce more carboxyl group and the successful introduction of Fe to yield CDs-Fe. Furthermore, high-resolution XPS spectra clearly exhibit the presence of different valence states of Fe and different Fe-binding sites with oxygen and nitrogen atoms. In high-resolution Fe2p XPS spectrum for CDs-Fe (Fig. 1D), two pairs of characteristic peaks are assigned to Fe2+ 2p3/2 (710.3 eV), Fe3+ 2p3/2 (714.0 eV), Fe2+ 2p1/2 (723.7 eV), and Fe3+ 2p1/2 (727.6 eV), indicating the existence of Fe2+ and Fe3+. The appearance of Fe2+ suggests the partial reduction of Fe3+ by CDs. Moreover, two satellite peaks at 730.0 and 717.3 eV exhibit the Fe–O and Fe–N structures, respectively [43]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum for CDs-Fe was fitted with three characteristic peaks at 532.9, 532.2, and 531.3, arising from C–O, CO, and Fe–O bonds (Fig. 1E). Moreover, the high-resolution N1s spectrum for CDs-Fe consists of four typical peaks at 403.3, 401.9, 401.2, and 399.9 eV, which can be attributed to graphitic N, pyrrolic N, Fe–N, and pyridinic N (Fig. 1F). Compared with high-resolution O1s (532.6 and 532.7 eV for C–O and CO bonds) and N1s (400.4, 399.4, and 399.0 eV for graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N) spectra for CDs (Fig. 1E and F), the binding energies in CDs-Fe are higher because Fe coordinates with surface oxygen and nitrogen-rich functional groups and decreases their electron-cloud densities. In addition, by comparison with the UV–vis and fluorescent spectra of CDs (Fig. S2), stronger absorption band among 400–600 nm, blue-shifted absorption band at 216 nm for π→π* transition, blue-shifted fluorescent emission at 445 nm, and the disappeared fluorescent emission at 660 nm for CDs-Fe exhibit the change of electron distribution by metal-ligand covalent [42], which further confirms the redox and coordination chemistry between CDs and Fe species to produce CDs-Fe.

Since the first report of Fe3O4 nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetics [25], almost all the peroxidase mimics are restricted to acidic conditions [1,26]. What is more, peroxidase mimics always have oxidase-like activity, inevitably affecting the detection accuracy because of the uncontrollable and indelible O2 in detection [26]. In recent years, many efforts have been made to fabrication and synthesis of peroxidase mimics with acceptable catalytic activity at neutral pH, however, as far as we are aware, successful examples are still rare [44]. Here, the enzyme-like performance of CDs-Fe was performed using TMB as a model chromogenic substrate. Obviously in Fig. 2A, no absorption peaks and blue color can be found for CDs-Fe, CDs-Fe + H2O2, CDs-Fe + TMB, or TMB + H2O2 solutions. Only CDs-Fe + TMB + H2O2 system displays the characteristic absorption enhancement at 652 nm for oxTMB and presents a distinct blue color (inset in Fig. 2A). Thus, CDs-Fe possesses high peroxidase-like activity without oxidase-mimetic activity, excluding the interference of O2, which can achieve more reliable results in quantitative analysis experiments. In sharp contrast, other CDs-metal ion composites (i.e., CDs-Fe2+, CDs-Cu2+, CDs-Mn2+, CDs-Co2+, CDs-Ni2+, and CDs-Ce3+) were prepared, and they exhibit poor peroxidase-like activity in the same experimental conditions (Fig. 2B and S3A). In addition, adding Na2EDTA (a strong metal chelating agent) into CDs-Fe catalytic reaction system, hardly any catalytic activity can be found (Fig. S3B). Thus, it can be concluded that Fe3+/Fe2+ species are the dominant active sites. Furthermore, some other biomass (i.e., mulberry, senna, mango, and peony leaves) derived CDs-Fe composites were prepared using the same procedures with those of aloe derived CDs-Fe. Interestingly in Fig. S3C, only aloe derived CDs-Fe with Fe3+/Fe2+ species presents high peroxidase-like activity. What's more, CDs alone can seldom catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, while FeCDs (synthesized by one-pot doping Fe3+ into CDs) and Fe species (the same content and composition with that in CDs-Fe) exhibits an inefficient peroxidase-like activity (Fig. S3D). Therefore, the excellent peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe might come from the cooperative effect between CDs and Fe species.

Similar to natural peroxidase, peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe is sensitive to the concentration of CDs-Fe (Fig. S4A), temperature (Fig. S4B), and working pH (Fig. 2C and S5A). The peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe increases with the increasement of the concentration of CDs-Fe, and the maximum activity can be achieved with working temperature and pH at 37 °C and 4.0. Notably, CDs-Fe presents superior peroxidase-like activity in a wide range of pH from 3.0 to 8.0, and also shows an excellent activity at pH 7.0 (52.5 % of that under pH 4.0) (Fig. 2C). Typically, during pH 3.0–9.0, CDs-Fe cannot catalytically oxidize TMB with the help of dissolved O2 (Fig. 2C and S5B). Then, synthesis parameters for CDs-Fe were optimized, and CDs-Fe with higher peroxidase-like activity at neutral pH was achieved when Fe3+ concentration and reaction time were set at 1.5 mM and 3 h (Fig. S6). Afterward, the storage stability of CDs-Fe as peroxidase mimics has been investigated (Fig. 2D). Notably, negligible peroxidase-like activity loss is found upon storage of CDs-Fe for 6 months, denoting the high stability. Therefore, the unique peroxidase-like activity and high stability of CDs-Fe at neutral pH makes it to be an ideal candidate in (bio)sensing analysis.

To gain more details about the peroxidase-like catalytic properties of CDs-Fe, the steady-state kinetics experiments were carried out by varying the concentrations of TMB or H2O2 at neutral pH. In brief, the kinetics data of CDs-Fe comply with the Michaelis−Menten and Lineweaver−Burk models in a concentration-dependent mode (Fig. 2E and F). Using TMB as the substrate, the K
m and v max values have been calculated to be 3.30 mM and 6.68 × 10−7 M•s−1. While for H2O2 as the substrate, the K
m and v max are 4.71 mM and 3.00 × 10−7 M•s−1, respectively. As is well known, K
m value is negatively related to the affinity of nanozymes to substrate, whereas v max is positively correlated with the catalytic ability of nanozymes [1,44]. The K
m of CDs-Fe for TMB is lower than that for H2O2, implying the higher affinity of CDs-Fe to TMB. Among the reported nanozymes with peroxidase-like activity at neutral pH (Table S1), K
m values of CDs-Fe for TMB and H2O2 are comparable with or lower than some reported nanozymes, suggesting the high affinity of CDs-Fe to substrates for activation. More interestingly, v max values of CDs-Fe are larger than most of the reported nanozymes (Table S1), implying the superior catalytic performance of CDs-Fe. The cooperation between CDs and Fe would account for the outstanding catalytic activity of CDs-Fe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of CDs-Fe nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies reported that peroxidase-like catalytic pathway of Fe-based nanozymes could be ascribed to the Fe2+ species as Fenton's reagent to generate the reactive hydroxyl substance (•OH) [26], and that of some carbon-based nanozymes might come from the electron transfer process [1]. Thus, we speculate that the peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of CDs-Fe might be the synergistic effect of the generation of •OH and electron transfer process.

To verify the production of •OH in CDs-Fe catalytic process, the reliable and direct electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiment with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trap of •OH was conducted. As exhibited in Fig. 3 A, a typical EPR signal with the peak intensity ratio at 1 : 2: 2 : 1 is observed in the DMPO + CDs-Fe + H2O2 system under neutral pH, indicating the formation of DMPO-OH spin adduct. In addition, various reactive oxygen species scavengers, thiourea for •OH, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) for O2 •‒, and l-tryptophan (TRP) for 1O2, were introduced to investigate the generated active free radicals (Fig. S7A). Obviously, •OH is the main active free radical generated in the process of CDs-Fe catalytic decomposition of H2O2. Likewise, the produced •OH involved in this catalytic system was also detected by methylene blue degradation-based colorimetric analysis. As expected in Fig. S7B, with the addition of methylene blue, the characteristic absorption for methylene blue at 665 nm decreases markedly and the solution color fades from blue to colorless, implying the high •OH generation capability of CDs-Fe in Fenton-like reaction. The •OH can then capture electrons from TMB to yield oxTMB. On the other hand, to confirm the CDs-Fe mediated electron transfer process from TMB to H2O2, cytochrome c (Cyt c), an electron donor, was selected to investigate the electron receptivity of CDs-Fe. Interestingly, after adding Cyt c into CDs-Fe, two typical absorption peaks for reduced Cyt c at 525 and 548 nm disappear and a new absorption peak at 528 nm for oxidized Cyt c occurs (Fig. 3B), indicating the electron transfer from Cyt c to CDs-Fe. Analogically, it can be concluded the TMB would donate electrons to CDs-Fe, leading to the increased electron density of CDs-Fe. Subsequently, the electrons can be captured by coordinated Fe3+ to produce Fe2+. After that, the electrons located in Fe2+ are transferred to neighboring H2O2. At this time, Fe2+ is oxidized back to Fe3+ to form an effective Fe3+/Fe2+ redox cycle, which further speed up the electron transfer process from TMB to CDs-Fe. During the CDs-Fe mediated electron transfer process, TMB can be rapidly oxidized into oxTMB and H2O2 is reduced to H2O. Taken together, two peroxidase-like catalytic pathways for CDs-Fe can be reasonably deduced as: (a) catalyzing H2O2 into •OH to oxidize TMB into oxTMB from Fenton-like reaction, and (b) serving as an electron acceptor to transfer electron from TMB to H2O2 (Fig. 3C). The synergistic effect boosts the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of CDs-Fe, which is consistent with the reported literatures [45–46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced catalytic mechanism of CDs-Fe nanozyme by dichlorvos
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe was performed after incubation with dichlorvos, and the results in Fig. 4 A show that obvious enhanced absorbance at 652 nm is observed with the addition of dichlorvos with concentration of 300 μg L−1 (19.1 times that of CDs-Fe + H2O2 + TMB system). Then a series of pesticides were selected for investigation, such as the phosphonate ester analogy (naled, glyphosate), thiophosphonate ester derivatives (quinaphos, phosemet, parathion-methyl (MP), parathion, phosphonothioic acid (EPN)), pyrethroid substance (transfluthrin), and carbamate derivatives (thiophanate-methyl (TM), carbendazim) (Fig. S8). Interestingly, except for dichlorvos, other pesticides do not exhibit obvious enhanced effect to the peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe (Fig. 4B and S9A), indicating the specific and efficient enhanced peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe by addition of dichlorvos. Thus, the enhancement mechanism was investigated.

The steady-state kinetics assays were firstly carried out, and the obtained curves of CDs-Fe/dichlorvos comply with the typical Michaelis–Menten equation, which confirms the peroxidase-like behavior (Figs. S10A and B). The enzymatic parameter K
m values were calculated to be 0.11 mM and 1.08 mM for TMB and H2O2 as substrates (Table S1), which are 30 and 4.3-fold lower than those of CDs-Fe, respectively, suggesting that the introduction of dichlorvos will largely improve the affinity to substrates. Furthermore, according to the standardized assay protocol [48], CDs-Fe/dichlorvos exhibits a specific peroxidase-mimic activity of 0.655 U mg−1, 1.28-fold higher than that of CDs-Fe (0.508 U mg−1) (Fig. S11), highlighting the crucial role of dichlorvos in enhancing catalytic activity. In order to illustrate the interactions among CDs-Fe, dichlorvos, and TMB, their FTIR spectra and Zeta potentials were investigated and compared. As illustrated in Figs. 4C and 1B, absorption peaks of N–H/O–H, C–H, and Fe–O for CDs-Fe and CDs-Fe/dichlorvos are almost the same. However, upon addition of dichlorvos, the stretching vibration peak of PO at 1278 cm−1 from dichlorvos blue-shifts to 1256 cm−1, and the CC/CN vibration peak at 1578 cm−1 from CDs-Fe red-shifts to 1595 cm−1. Meanwhile, Zeta potentials of CDs-Fe and dichlorvos are determined as −25.10 and + 9.01, respectively (Fig. 4D). The difference illustrates that a complex might be formed between CDs-Fe and dichlorvos via electrostatic attraction and π-π stacking interactions. It can be concluded that, at neutral pH, negative charged TMB (−3.95 mV) can be easily absorbed on the surface of positive charged CDs-Fe/dichlorvos (+5.54 mV) through electrostatic attraction interaction (Fig. 4D), which will play an important role in enhancing the peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe. Additionally, dichlorvos can decrease the fluorescent emission of CDs-Fe (Fig. S9B), which might come from the electron transfer process [47]. Then we speculate that dichlorvos would also be benefit for electron transfer in the peroxidase-like process. After addition of different amount of dichlorvos with CDs-Fe, the catalytic ability of the system was studied by measuring the current response change of the TMB utilizing cyclic voltammograms (CV) (Fig. 4E). The peak current of oxTMB significantly increase from 2.65 μA to 18.1 μA with the dichlorvos concentration increasing from 0 to 500 μg L−1, indicating that the addition of dichlorvos will promote the oxidation of TMB. Moreover, the smaller Nyquist radius arc for CDs-Fe/dichlorvos (Fig. S9C) presents the smaller electron transfer resistance. As a result, the addition of dichlorvos will promote the efficiency of electron transport from TMB to H2O2. Accordingly, the complex between dichlorvos and CDs-Fe would cement substrates affinity and improve electron transfer efficiency (Fig. 4F), which then enhance the catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal analysis of dichlorvos by CDs-Fe based portable hydrogel kit
ce:section.ce:para: There is an urgent need to fabricate portable device for on-site analysis of dichlorvos to evaluate food safety and environmental poisoning [22]. Agarose hydrogel presents 3D cross-linked macromolecular networks with mechanical stability, flexibility, favorable biocompatibility, easy storage, and no toxic cross-linking agents, which can be selected as a robust carrier to prepare point-of-care testing platforms [49]. Here, CDs-Fe and TMB were embedded in an agarose hydrogel matrix into commercial 96-arrays microplate to develop a novel portable kit for the convenient, rapid, and specific detection of dichlorvos (Fig. 5 A). The freeze-dried hydrogel shows the interconnected porosity structure (Fig. S12A), allowing for the excellent loading of CDs-Fe and TMB and the reaction system for dichlorvos detection. Specially, under 808 nm laser irradiation, oxTMB presents superior photothermal conversion capacity [23], which allows the conversion of the information of the dichlorvos concentration to a visible thermal signal for photothermal analysis.

According to the design, temperature changes of different reaction systems were firstly tested under 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. S12B). By using the near-infrared imaging camera, we can not only achieve the color images related to different temperatures but also read the accurate temperatures displayed on computer. Obviously, hydrogel without and with CDs-Fe + TMB or CDs-Fe + TMB + dichlorvos hardly possess photothermal effect, whereas hydrogel with TMB + CDs-Fe + H2O2 system can produce oxTMB and has an obvious temperature elevation, suggesting that the strong photothermal effect originates from oxTMB. Specifically, CDs-Fe + TMB + dichlorvos + H2O2 system exhibits a significant temperature increment. It can be attributed to the dichlorvos-mediated enhancement of peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe to generate more oxTMB, which is the same with those observed in colorimetric assay in Fig. 4A. Therefore, we believe that photothermal detection of dichlorvos can be developed. Subsequently, the influence of irradiation time on photothermal effect was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. S12C, the optimal irradiation time can be set at 2.5 min. Furthermore, the stability of the CDs-Fe based portable hydrogel kit for photothermal detection of dichlorvos was assayed. As evidenced from Fig. S12D, under an 808 nm laser irradiation for five heating/cooling cycles, CDs-Fe + TMB + dichlorvos + H2O2 system maintain an excellent and stable photothermal conversion performance, showing the potential reuse merits. Furtherly, the temperature variations of the dichlorvos detection system are seldom affected during 24 h reaction period (Fig. S12E) and sealed storing the portable kit at 4 °C for 7 days (Fig. S12F), demonstrating a stable and reliable temperature signal readout for photothermal detection of dichlorvos.

To obtain the excellent performance of the proposed photothermal detection platform, reaction conditions, such as incubation time after addition of dichlorvos and H2O2 (20 min), the concentrations of CDs-Fe (30 μg mL−1), TMB (0.5 mM), and H2O2 (5 mM) were investigated and optimized (Fig. S13). Under the optimized parameters, the concentration of dichlorvos can be quantitatively analyzed by monitoring the photothermal behavior. Fig. 5B indicates that the ΔT (ΔT = T ‒ T0, where T and T0 represent the temperature of reaction systems with and without 808 nm laser irradiation) elevates linearly with the concentration of dichlorvos increasement from 5.00 to 650 μg L−1 (y = 0.0523 x + 25.9541, R2 = 0.9921). The limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/S, σ and S are the standard deviation of blank samples and the slope of linear equation, n = 11) of the photothermal method for dichlorvos detection was determined to be 4.85 μg L−1. By comparison with previously reported fluorescent, colorimetric, and photothermal methods (Table S2), the fabricated photothermal detection platform exhibits excellent detection performance, rapid, simple, and specific detection property for dichlorvos. In addition, to evaluate the specificity of the photothermal detection system to dichlorvos, a series of common interferences, such as metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+), amino acids (l-proline, l-leucine, l-valine, l-serine), and pesticides (glyphosate, quinaphos, phosemet, MP, parathion, EPN, TM, carbendazim) were selected. As clearly shown in Fig. 5C, after addition of potential interferences (1000 μg L−1) into the CDs-Fe + TMB + dichlorvos (300 μg L−1) + H2O2 system, the temperature presents little apparent change, indicating the satisfactory anti-interference ability to dichlorvos. This was much better than bioenzyme inhibition-based method for pesticides detection, which had response to a group of pesticides [22]. Furthermore, the reproducibility was tested (Table S3), for five samples with the same concentration of dichlorvos, accurate detection results with high precision (relative standard deviation (RSD), 4.89 % and 7.59 % for intra-day and inter-day variations) have been obtained. Thus, the constructed photothermal detection platform for dichlorvos presents satisfactory stability, excellent selectivity, superior repeatability, and high sensitivity, which is appropriate for on-site practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of dichlorvos in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the high dichlorvos residual risk in vegetables, lettuce leaves and spinach leaves were chosen as real samples to evaluate the practicability and reliability of the proposed CDs-Fe based portable hydrogel kit. Fig. 5D exhibits the color images and photothermal images of lettuce leaves extract in the CDs-Fe based portable hydrogel kit with different dilution times. Obviously, green color of lettuce leaves extract for pigments (e.g., chlorophyll) is observed, and with the increasement of dilution time, the color gradually declines, while the color is also clearly found in a 100-fold dilution. The UV–vis absorption spectra also present the characteristic peaks for chlorophyll (Fig. S14A), and the absorbance around 652 nm can be detected with the dilution time increasing to 100 times, resulting in significant background interferences for colorimetric analysis. Notably, chlorophyll has no absorption peak at about 808 nm (Fig. S14A), thus no photothermal interferences can be found under 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 5D). Furthermore, the colorimetric and photothermal detection results were studied after addition of extract solution with dichlorvos (300 μg L−1) (Fig. 5E). For addition of dichlorvos standard solution, a good linear relationship can also be acquired between B/(R + B) ratio and dichlorvos concentration (y = 0.0004x + 0.5108, R 2 = 0.9941) in the range of 5.00–650 μg L−1 (Fig. S14B). While for addition of extract solution, the calculated concentrations for colorimetric detection are largely less than 300 μg L−1 until the dilution time reaches 100 times because of the existence of background interferences (Fig. 5E). It is noted that little influence is observed for photothermal analysis (Fig. 5E). Therefore, the proposed CDs-Fe based portable hydrogel kit was quite qualified for dichlorvos in real samples without background interferences, complex sample pretreatment process, and expensive instruments.

As presented in Table S4, no dichlorvos is detected in lettuce leaves and spinach leaves. The recoveries for spiked samples are 90.56–107.0 % with RSD less than 4.67 %, indeed demonstrating excellent reliability, accuracy, and precision. Moreover, the photothermal detection results keep consistent with the values analyzed by HPLC–MS, justifying the good practicability. Taken together, the proposed method supplies a promising avenue for onsite sensitive, accurate, specific, and background-free detection of dichlorvos in real samples, offering outstanding protocol to ensure food safety.

To sum up, we proposed a new photothermal assay for on-site direct, sensitive, specific, and background-free detection of dichlorvos based on the nanozyme enhancement strategy. Our strategy demonstrated several advantages: (a) CDs-Fe features excellent peroxidase-like activity but not oxidase-like activity at neutral pH, which will help to design rational neutral catalytic reaction systems for efficient (bio)sensing applications; (b) it can directly and specifically detect dichlorvos based on the distinctive enhancement effect on the peroxidase-like activity of CDs-Fe; (c) photothermal analysis can eliminate the background interferences and achieve the dichlorvos analysis in real samples with high sensitivity and accuracy; (d) CDs-Fe based portable hydrogel kit was simply fabricated and used for rapid and point-of-care testing of dichlorvos only with the assistance of laser and thermal imaging camera. Overall, the current strategy will set up new avenues to fabricate novel nanozymes, improve their analytical performance, and broaden their application scopes.
